guidedCookSteps.instruction: Spread in a shallow bowl, swirl with extra olive oil, and top with chopped parsley and a few pomegranate seeds if you have them. Serve with warm pita.

name: Fattoush
description: A Levantine bread salad with crispy pita shards, fresh vegetables, herbs, and a tangy sumac-lemon dressing.
pairingReason: crispy pita salad for crunch and tang

name: Stuffed Grape Leaves
description: Tender vine leaves wrapped around a fragrant filling of herbed rice, pine nuts, and a splash of lemon juice.
pairingReason: delicate herbed bites for a traditional spread

name: Tzatziki
description: A refreshing Greek dip of thick strained yogurt, grated cucumber, garlic, dill, and a drizzle of olive oil.
pairingReason: cool cucumber dip to tame grilled meats

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Grate 1 medium English cucumber (about 1 cup grated) on the large holes of a box grater. You don't need to peel it — the skin adds color and nutrition.
Place the grated cucumber in a clean kitchen towel or fine-mesh sieve. Sprinkle with 1/2 teaspoon salt, let sit 10 minutes, then squeeze firmly over the sink until almost no liquid comes out.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the squeeze is the #1 tzatziki mistake. Wet cucumber makes watery sauce within an hour. Squeeze until it feels dry.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: A potato ricer works incredibly well to squeeze cucumber — more efficient than a towel.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: For extra-thick tzatziki, line a fine strainer with cheesecloth or a paper towel and let 2 cups of full-fat Greek yogurt drain over a bowl in the fridge for 1 hour (or skip if you're using a very thick yogurt like Fage Total 5%).
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Low-fat or regular (non-Greek) yogurt will make thin, watery tzatziki. Use whole-milk Greek yogurt.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Authentic Greek tzatziki traditionally uses strained sheep or goat yogurt — hence why Greek-style (strained) yogurt is the closest substitute.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a bowl, combine the yogurt, squeezed cucumber, 2 finely minced garlic cloves, 1 tablespoon extra virgin olive oil, 1 tablespoon red wine vinegar or fresh lemon juice, and 2 tablespoons finely chopped fresh dill (or mint).
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't go overboard on garlic — 2 cloves is plenty. It'll get stronger as it sits.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: For mellower garlic, grate it on a microplane and let it mingle with the lemon juice for 5 minutes before adding yogurt — the acid tames the bite.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Season with 1/2 teaspoon kosher salt and black pepper. Cover and refrigerate at least 30 minutes (overnight is better) to let flavors meld. Drizzle with olive oil before serving.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Tzatziki tastes raw and harsh straight after mixing. That 30-minute rest is non-negotiable — it's when the sauce comes together.

name: Pita Chips
description: Pita bread cut into triangles and baked until golden and crunchy, seasoned with olive oil, salt, and za'atar.
pairingReason: crunchy za'atar chips for scooping dips

name: Pickled Turnips
description: Vibrant pink-hued turnips pickled in vinegar and beet juice, offering a crunchy, tangy contrast to rich dishes.
pairingReason: tangy pickle to cut through fatty, rich bites

name: Spring Rolls
description: Crispy, golden-fried rolls filled with vegetables and glass noodles, served with a sweet chilli dipping sauce.
pairingReason: crispy fried starter to open a Thai meal

name: Tom Yum Soup
description: A hot-and-sour Thai soup bursting with lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime, prawns, and fiery bird's eye chillies.
pairingReason: hot-sour broth to awaken the appetite

name: Papaya Salad
description: Shredded green papaya pounded with tomatoes, peanuts, dried shrimp, lime, and chilli in a spicy-sour dressing.
pairingReason: spicy-sour crunch to contrast noodle dishes

name: Thai Iced Tea
description: A creamy, bright-orange iced tea made from strongly brewed Ceylon tea, sweetened condensed milk, and crushed ice.
pairingReason: sweet, creamy drink to cool spicy heat

name: Satay
description: Tender, marinated chicken or pork skewers grilled over charcoal and served with a rich, spiced peanut dipping sauce.
pairingReason: chargrilled skewers with nutty peanut sauce

name: Sticky Rice
description: Glutinous rice steamed until beautifully sticky and chewy, the perfect vessel for soaking up rich Thai curries and sauces.
pairingReason: chewy, sticky rice to absorb bold sauces

name: Larb
description: A zesty Thai minced meat salad seasoned with lime, fish sauce, toasted rice powder, chilli, and fresh herbs.
pairingReason: zesty meat salad for a punch of herbs

name: Coconut Soup
description: A silky, aromatic soup made with creamy coconut milk, galangal, lemongrass, mushrooms, and a hint of lime.
pairingReason: velvety coconut broth to round out spice

name: Pickled Ginger
description: Paper-thin slices of young ginger pickled in sweet rice vinegar, turning a delicate pink — a palate cleanser between bites.
pairingReason: palate cleanser between different flavours

name: French Fries
description: Twice-fried potato sticks with a shattering golden crust and fluffy interior, generously seasoned with salt.
pairingReason: salty, crispy classic alongside burgers

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Peel 2 lbs (about 4) russet potatoes. Cut into ¼-inch by ¼-inch sticks — uniform thickness is crucial for even cooking. Drop the sticks into a bowl of cold water as you go to prevent browning.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Using waxy potatoes (red, Yukon) makes soggy fries. Russets have the right starch content.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Square off the potato first by cutting a thin slab off each side — this gives you uniform sticks instead of awkward curved pieces.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Drain the potatoes and place them in a large pot. Cover with cold water by 1 inch, add 2 Tbsp white vinegar and 2 Tbsp kosher salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a bare simmer for 10 minutes until the fries are nearly tender but hold their shape.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the vinegar makes fries that turn to mush in the boil. The acid keeps the pectin firm.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: This boiled-then-fried method is Kenji's breakthrough — it gelatinizes the surface starches so the fries crisp up instantly when fried.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Drain the fries gently and spread them on a paper-towel-lined baking sheet. Pat dry. Let them air-dry for 5 minutes — surface moisture is the enemy of crispness.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Wet fries make the oil splatter dangerously and create a soggy crust.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Heat 2 quarts of peanut or canola oil in a Dutch oven to 400°F (use a thermometer). Working in 3 batches, fry the potatoes for 50 seconds per batch — they should be pale and limp when removed. Let oil return to 400°F between batches.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Crowding the oil drops the temperature and gives you greasy fries. Three batches, no shortcuts.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: After this first fry, you can refrigerate the fries for up to a day before the second fry — perfect for entertaining.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Increase oil temperature to 400°F. Fry the potatoes a second time, all together this time, until deep golden brown and crispy, 3–4 minutes. Stir gently with a spider to prevent sticking.
Transfer fries to a paper-towel-lined bowl, immediately season with kosher salt while shiny with oil so the salt sticks. Toss to coat. Serve right now — fries lose their crisp within 5 minutes.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Salting after they cool means the salt slides off. Salt while hot.

name: Onion Rings
description: Thick-cut onion slices dipped in seasoned batter and fried until the coating turns crispy and deep golden brown.
pairingReason: sweet-savoury crunch for a diner-style pairing

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Slice 2 large yellow onions into ½-inch-thick rounds. Separate into individual rings, discarding the small inner rings (or save them for soup). You should have about 24 large rings.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Slicing thinner than ½ inch gives you flimsy rings that overcook before the crust browns.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Use sweet onions like Vidalia for a milder flavor that lets the crust shine.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Soak the onion rings in 2 cups buttermilk with 1 tsp salt for 30 minutes. The buttermilk tenderizes and adds a subtle tang while the salt seasons the onion itself.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: No buttermilk? Stir 2 Tbsp lemon juice into 2 cups whole milk and let it sit 5 minutes.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a large bowl, whisk together 2 cups all-purpose flour, ½ cup cornstarch, 1 Tbsp baking powder, 2 tsp salt, 1 tsp paprika, and ½ tsp cayenne. The cornstarch is the secret to extra crunch.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: ATK's test was clear: cornstarch + flour beats flour alone every time. The cornstarch fries up shatter-crisp.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Heat 2 quarts of vegetable oil in a Dutch oven to exactly 375°F. A thermometer is non-negotiable — too low and the rings absorb oil; too high and they burn before cooking through.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Eyeballing oil temperature is the #1 onion ring failure. Get a clip-on thermometer.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Working in batches, lift rings from buttermilk, let excess drip off, then dredge in the flour mixture, pressing firmly so the coating sticks. Re-dip in buttermilk and dredge again for a double coat.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the double-dredge gives you a thin crust that falls off in the oil.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: ATK shakes all the rings together with the flour mix in a paper grocery bag — faster and more even than dredging one by one.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Lower 6–8 rings into the oil with tongs. Fry without stirring for 30–60 seconds to set the crust, then stir gently and continue frying until dark golden brown, 3–5 minutes total.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Stirring too early knocks off the wet coating before it can set.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Transfer to a wire rack set over a baking sheet (not paper towels — they trap steam and soften the crust). Sprinkle with flaky salt immediately. Repeat with remaining rings, returning oil to 375°F between batches.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Resting hot fried food on paper towels is how you ruin onion rings. Always use a wire rack.

name: Sweet Potato Fries
description: Crispy-on-the-outside, soft-inside sweet potato sticks with a natural caramel sweetness and a hint of sea salt.
pairingReason: natural sweetness for a lighter fry option

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Preheat the oven to 425°F and line two large baking sheets with parchment paper. Hot oven + two pans (so fries have room) is the foundation of crispy oven fries.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Crowding fries onto one pan steams them. Two pans is non-negotiable for crispness.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Peel 2 lbs sweet potatoes (about 2 large) and cut into matchsticks ¼-inch thick on each side. Uniform size = uniform crispness.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Cutting fries thicker than ¼ inch leaves them soft inside while the outside burns.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Square off the round sides of the potato first — easier to get straight cuts from a flat surface.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Soak the cut fries in a bowl of cold water for at least 30 minutes (up to 2 hours). This pulls out surface starches that would otherwise turn gummy.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the soak is the #1 reason home-baked sweet potato fries come out limp.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Drain the fries and dry them THOROUGHLY with kitchen towels — pat each one until no water remains. Toss in a dry bowl with 2 Tbsp cornstarch until every fry is lightly coated.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Wet fries + cornstarch = paste. Dry first, cornstarch second.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: The order matters: oil only AFTER the cornstarch coats. Cornstarch on dry fries → then drizzle 2 Tbsp oil → toss to coat.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Drizzle 2 Tbsp olive oil over the cornstarched fries and toss to coat evenly. Season with 1 tsp salt, ½ tsp garlic powder, and ½ tsp smoked paprika. Spread in a single layer across both baking sheets — fries should not touch.
Bake on the center and lower racks for 20 minutes. Pull both pans, flip every fry with a thin metal spatula, and rotate the pans (top to bottom, front to back).
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: A thin metal spatula slides under without ripping the cornstarch coating.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Return to the oven and bake another 10–15 minutes, until the fries are deep golden brown at the edges and crisp. Watch the last 5 minutes carefully — sweet potatoes go from perfect to burnt fast.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Sweet potatoes have more sugar than russets and burn quickly. Stay close at the end.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Let the fries cool on the pan for 3–5 minutes — they continue to crisp as they cool. Transfer to a serving plate and add a final sprinkle of flaky salt.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Serve with chipotle aioli: ½ cup mayo + 1 minced chipotle in adobo + squeeze of lime.

name: Potato Salad
description: Tender potato chunks folded with mayo, mustard, crunchy celery, hard-boiled eggs, and a scatter of fresh dill.
pairingReason: creamy potato side for outdoor cookouts

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Peel 2 lbs Yukon Gold potatoes and cut into ¾-inch cubes. Place directly into a large pot and cover with cold water by 1 inch. Add 2 Tbsp white vinegar and 2 Tbsp kosher salt.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Starting potatoes in hot water cooks the outsides before the centers — always start in cold water for even cooking.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Vinegar in the boiling water keeps the pectin firm so cubes hold their shape instead of crumbling.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Bring the pot to a boil over high heat, then reduce to a steady simmer. Cook until a paring knife slides into a cube with no resistance, 12–15 minutes.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Boiling too hard knocks the cubes against each other and shears the edges into a mushy paste. Keep it at a gentle simmer.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Drain the potatoes in a colander. While still hot, toss them gently in a wide bowl with 2 Tbsp white wine vinegar — hot potatoes drink up the seasoning. Let cool 10 minutes.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the hot vinegar dousing leaves bland potatoes that no amount of dressing later can fix.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: This step is Kenji's key insight: season at every layer, not just at the end.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: While potatoes cool, finely dice 3 ribs of celery, 4 scallions (whites and greens separated), and 4 hard-boiled eggs. Roughly chop ⅓ cup cornichons or dill pickles.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Hard-boil eggs by placing in cold water, bringing to a boil, covering, turning off heat, and waiting 11 minutes. Ice bath, then peel.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a large bowl, whisk ¾ cup mayonnaise, 2 Tbsp Dijon mustard, 2 Tbsp sweet pickle relish, 1 tsp celery seed, ½ tsp salt, and freshly ground black pepper. Fold in the cooled potatoes, celery, scallion whites, eggs, and pickles.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Fold gently with a rubber spatula, lifting from the bottom — stirring smashes the cubes.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Refrigerate for at least 1 hour. Just before serving, taste and adjust salt and pepper, then top with the reserved scallion greens and a sprinkle of paprika.

name: Cornbread
description: Warm, golden cornbread with a slightly sweet crumb and a buttery crust, baked in a cast-iron skillet.
pairingReason: sweet, crumbly bread to mop up barbecue sauce

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Place a 10-inch cast iron skillet in the cold oven and preheat to 425°F. The screaming-hot pan is what gives Southern cornbread its signature crackly crust.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Pouring batter into a cold or barely warm skillet is the #1 reason home cornbread is pale and pasty on the bottom.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: No cast iron? A heavy oven-safe skillet works, but the crust won't be as dramatic.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Cast iron is the traditional Southern vessel for cornbread because the thermal mass mimics a hearth oven.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a large bowl, whisk together 1½ cups yellow cornmeal, ½ cup all-purpose flour, 2 Tbsp sugar (or honey), 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp baking soda, and 1 tsp baking powder.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the baking soda gives you flat, dense cornbread — buttermilk needs both soda (for rise) and powder (for lift).
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Use stone-ground cornmeal for a more rustic, full-corn flavor and slightly coarser crumb.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a separate bowl, whisk 2 large eggs with 1¾ cups buttermilk and ¼ cup melted unsalted butter (cooled slightly so it doesn't scramble the eggs).
Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and stir with a rubber spatula just until no streaks of flour remain. Some lumps are fine — overmixing develops gluten and gives you tough cornbread.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Stirring until perfectly smooth = chewy, dense bread. Stop the moment everything is moistened.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Pull the hot skillet from the oven (use thick mitts!). Add 2 Tbsp butter — it will sizzle and foam. Swirl to coat the bottom and sides, then immediately pour in the batter. It should sizzle on contact.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: If the butter doesn't sizzle when it hits the pan, the pan isn't hot enough — you'll lose the crust.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Substitute the butter with 2 Tbsp bacon fat for the truly authentic Southern flavor.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Return the skillet to the oven and bake until the top is golden brown and a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean, 20–25 minutes.
Let the cornbread rest in the skillet for 5 minutes before slicing into wedges. Serve warm with butter and honey.

name: Baked Beans
description: Navy beans slow-baked in a smoky, sweet molasses and tomato sauce with bits of bacon and brown sugar.
pairingReason: smoky-sweet beans for a classic barbecue spread

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Soak 1 lb dried navy beans in 8 cups cold water overnight (at least 8 hours). They'll roughly double in size.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the overnight soak adds 1+ hours to the cooking time and gives you unevenly cooked beans.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: No time to soak overnight? Quick-soak: cover beans with water, boil 2 minutes, cover, off heat, sit 1 hour.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Boston Baked Beans built American molasses culture — colonial New England traded heavily in West Indies molasses.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Drain and rinse the beans. Place in a Dutch oven with 8 cups fresh water. Bring to a boil, reduce to a simmer, and cook uncovered until tender but still holding shape, about 60 minutes. Drain, reserving 2 cups of the cooking liquid.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Adding salt or acid (tomato, vinegar) before the beans are tender keeps them tough forever. Save those for after.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: While beans simmer, cut 8 oz thick-cut bacon into ½-inch pieces. In the same Dutch oven (after draining beans), fry the bacon over medium heat until the fat is rendered and bacon is just crisp, 6–8 minutes. Remove bacon to a paper towel; leave fat in the pot.
In the bacon fat, sauté 1 large diced yellow onion until softened and golden at the edges, 6–8 minutes. Add 2 minced garlic cloves and cook 30 seconds more.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Don't rush the onions — the deeper they brown here, the deeper the finished bean flavor.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Stir in ½ cup fancy molasses (NOT blackstrap — too bitter), ⅓ cup brown sugar, ⅓ cup ketchup, 2 Tbsp apple cider vinegar, 2 Tbsp Dijon mustard, 1 tsp salt, and ½ tsp black pepper. Add the cooked beans, bacon, and 1½ cups of the reserved bean liquid. Stir to combine.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Blackstrap molasses turns baked beans bitter and metallic. Use "fancy" or "light" molasses (Grandma's, Crosby's).

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Cover the Dutch oven and transfer to a 350°F oven. Bake 2 hours, stirring every 30 minutes and adding more bean liquid (or water) if it looks dry.
Uncover and bake another 30 minutes, until the sauce is thick and glossy and a few beans on top are slightly caramelized. Let rest 10 minutes before serving — they thicken further off the heat.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Leftovers are even better day two — let them cool completely, then reheat with a splash of water.

name: Mac & Cheese
description: Elbow pasta swimming in a luscious, three-cheese sauce, topped with buttery breadcrumbs and baked until bubbly.
pairingReason: indulgent cheese comfort for hearty meals

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Grate 6 oz sharp cheddar (or a mix of cheddar + Gruyère) on the large holes of a box grater. Pre-grated bagged cheese has anti-caking starch that makes the sauce gritty.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Using bagged shredded cheese is the fastest way to ruin this recipe — the cellulose coating refuses to melt smoothly.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: A 50/50 mix of sharp cheddar and Gruyère gives you classic flavor + extra meltiness.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Measure 6 oz dried elbow macaroni and 6 oz (¾ cup) evaporated milk into separate small bowls. Have everything within arm's reach — once the cooking starts, it moves fast.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Use a kitchen scale — eyeballing 6 oz of dried pasta is hard, and ratios matter for this no-roux method.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Place the macaroni in a medium saucepan and add just enough cold water to cover (about 1½ cups). Add a generous pinch of salt. Bring to a boil over high heat, stirring frequently to prevent sticking.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Adding too much water dilutes the starch you need for a creamy sauce. Just barely cover.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: The starchy pasta water IS the sauce base in this method — that's why traditional "drain the pasta" rules don't apply here.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Continue cooking, stirring constantly, until most of the water has been absorbed and the pasta is just shy of al dente, about 6 minutes total. The pot should look almost dry.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Walking away during this stage will scorch the pasta on the bottom. Constant stirring is non-negotiable.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Pour in the evaporated milk and bring back to a simmer. Reduce heat to low and add the grated cheese a handful at a time, stirring continuously until each addition is fully melted before adding the next.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Dumping all the cheese in at once breaks the sauce. Add gradually for a perfectly emulsified, glossy cheese sauce.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: The proteins in evaporated milk are what stabilize this sauce — regular milk will break and turn grainy.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Stir in ½ tsp Dijon mustard, a few grinds of black pepper, and a pinch of cayenne for warmth. Taste and adjust salt. Serve immediately — this dish is best the moment it's done.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Dijon doesn't taste mustardy in the finished dish — it amplifies the cheese flavor.

name: Pickles
description: Crunchy dill pickle spears brined in vinegar, garlic, and dill — a tangy, refreshing palate cleanser.
pairingReason: sharp tang to cut through greasy, rich bites

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Wash 1½ lbs Kirby (pickling) cucumbers and trim about ⅛ inch off each end — the blossom end contains enzymes that soften pickles. Slice into ¼-inch coins or spears.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Regular slicing cucumbers (long, smooth-skinned) give you soft, hollow pickles. Always use Kirby or Persian.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: For maximum crunch, soak cut cucumbers in ice water for 30 minutes before brining.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a clean wide-mouth quart jar, layer in 4 smashed garlic cloves, 1 Tbsp whole black peppercorns, 1 tsp yellow mustard seeds, 1 tsp coriander seeds, ½ tsp red pepper flakes, and a generous handful of fresh dill (about ¼ cup of fronds + 2 dill flower heads if you have them).
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Smashing the garlic with the side of a knife (not mincing) releases flavor without making the pickles taste like garlic paste.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Pack the cucumbers into the jar, standing them on end if cut as spears, or layering coins. Pack tightly — they should not float freely.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Loosely packed pickles bob to the surface, dry out, and turn slimy.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a small saucepan, combine 1½ cups distilled white vinegar (5% acidity), 1½ cups water, 2 Tbsp kosher salt, and 1 Tbsp sugar. Bring to a boil over high heat, stirring until the salt and sugar fully dissolve, about 2 minutes.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Using a vinegar with less than 5% acidity won't safely preserve the pickles — check the label.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Remove brine from heat and let cool until just barely warm to the touch (about 15 minutes), or speed it up by setting the pot in an ice bath. Pouring boiling brine over cucumbers makes mushy pickles.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Hot brine = soft pickles, period. Patience here is the difference between snappy and sad.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Refrigerator pickles are NOT shelf-stable — they keep 4–6 weeks in the fridge but should never be left out.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Pour the cooled brine over the cucumbers until fully submerged. Tap the jar gently on the counter to release air bubbles. Seal and refrigerate at least 24 hours before tasting — they'll keep getting better for a week.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: For peak flavor, wait 3 full days. The garlic and dill need time to infuse.

name: Creamed Spinach
description: Tender spinach leaves wilted and folded into a rich, garlicky cream sauce with a hint of nutmeg.
pairingReason: rich, creamy greens alongside steak

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Bring a large pot of well-salted water to a boil. Blanch 1.5 lbs fresh baby spinach in batches for 30 seconds, then immediately transfer to an ice bath. Drain and squeeze HARD with your hands until almost no liquid comes out. Roughly chop. (Or use 16 oz frozen spinach, thawed and squeezed dry.)
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Failing to squeeze the spinach is the #1 reason creamed spinach turns watery and bland. Squeeze until your hands hurt.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: A potato ricer is the fastest way to wring water out of cooked spinach — load it in, press down, and you're done.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: American steakhouse creamed spinach descends from French épinards à la crème, which arrived in U.S. fine-dining via Delmonico's in the 19th century.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Melt 4 tablespoons unsalted butter in a large skillet over medium heat. Add 1 finely diced small yellow onion and a pinch of salt. Cook, stirring, until soft and translucent, about 4-5 minutes. Add 3 minced garlic cloves and cook 30 seconds until fragrant.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Burnt garlic turns bitter — add it AFTER the onion is soft, not at the start.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Sprinkle 3 tablespoons all-purpose flour over the onions and whisk constantly for 1-2 minutes until the mixture is foamy and smells nutty (but doesn't darken). This cooks out the raw flour taste.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the flour cook-off leaves a chalky, raw-flour aftertaste in the finished dish.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Slowly stream in 1 cup whole milk and 1/2 cup heavy cream while whisking constantly. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook, stirring often, until the sauce thickens and coats the back of a spoon — about 4-5 minutes.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Pouring in cold cream all at once causes lumps. Add it slowly and whisk constantly.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Stir in the squeezed spinach until evenly coated. Add 1/2 cup grated Parmesan, 1/4 teaspoon freshly grated nutmeg, 3/4 teaspoon kosher salt, and a few grinds of black pepper. Cook 2 more minutes to heat through.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't skip the nutmeg — it's the secret ingredient that turns plain creamed spinach into the steakhouse version.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: A few drops of fresh lemon juice at the end brightens the heavy sauce without making it taste lemony.

name: Baked Potato
description: A russet potato baked until the skin is crispy and the inside is fluffy, split open and loaded with butter and sour cream.
pairingReason: loaded baked potato for a hearty companion

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Preheat oven to 425°F. Scrub 4 large Russet potatoes (about 12 oz each) under cold water with a brush. Pat completely dry with a towel — wet skin won't crisp up.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't skip drying — moisture is the enemy of crispy skin.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Russets are the king for baked potatoes because their thick, starchy skin crisps best while the inside fluffs up.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Pierce each potato 6-8 times with a fork to let steam escape. Rub each one all over with about 1 teaspoon olive oil and sprinkle generously with kosher salt — really pack it on.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Wrapping in foil is the BIGGEST mistake — it traps steam, leaves the skin soft and limp instead of crispy. Bake naked.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: The salt isn't just for flavor — it draws moisture out and gives you that signature crusty, salty steakhouse skin.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Place potatoes directly on the middle oven rack (or on a foil-lined sheet pan to catch drips). Bake at 425°F for 50-65 minutes until a paring knife slides easily into the center and the skin is dry and crisp.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Pulling them too early leaves a hard, undercooked center. The skin will look done before the inside is fluffy.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: For peak fluff, an instant-read thermometer in the center should read 205-210°F.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Pull from the oven and let rest 5 minutes. Slash the top with an X using a knife and squeeze the ends gently to fluff the inside. The interior should burst out steamy and pillowy.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Don't slice straight down — the X-slash and squeeze technique lifts the flesh up and creates more surface area for butter to soak in.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Top with butter, sour cream, chives, sharp cheddar, crumbled bacon, or whatever you like. Always season the inside with a pinch more salt and fresh black pepper before serving — the inside is bland without it.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Forgetting to salt the fluffy inside is why a lot of homemade baked potatoes feel bland under loaded toppings.

name: Greek Salad
description: Juicy tomatoes, cucumber, red onion, Kalamata olives, and a slab of creamy feta, dressed in olive oil and oregano.
pairingReason: classic Mediterranean salad for freshness

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Choose the ripest tomatoes you can find — this salad lives or dies by the tomato. Cut 4 medium tomatoes into wedges or thick 1-inch chunks (not slices). Transfer to a shallow bowl.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Out-of-season pale tomatoes will make a sad Greek salad. If your tomatoes aren't great, cherry or grape tomatoes are a better substitute than mealy big tomatoes.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Sprinkle the cut tomatoes with a pinch of salt immediately — it draws out their juices, which become part of the dressing.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Authentic horiatiki has no lettuce — the 'Greek salad' with lettuce is a Western invention. In Greece, it's just tomato, cucumber, pepper, onion, olives, and feta.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Peel 1 English cucumber partially (leave stripes of skin for color), then slice in half lengthwise and cut into thick 1/2-inch half-moons. Add to the bowl with the tomatoes.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Thin slices turn soggy fast. Keep the cucumber chunky so it stays crisp.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Slice 1 small red onion into very thin half-moons. Core and slice 1 green bell pepper into thin rings. Add both to the bowl along with 1/2 cup Kalamata olives (pitted or whole — whole is traditional).
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Raw red onion can overpower the salad. If yours is sharp, soak the slices in cold water for 10 minutes to mellow them.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Drizzle the vegetables with 3-4 tablespoons good extra virgin olive oil and 1 tablespoon red wine vinegar. Season with 1/2 teaspoon kosher salt and a generous sprinkle of dried Greek oregano — crush it between your palms as you add it.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't skimp on the olive oil — it's the main dressing. Use the best you have.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Crushing the oregano between your palms wakes up the essential oils and releases way more aroma than just sprinkling.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Top the salad with a thick 1/2-inch slab of feta (about 4 oz), placed whole on top rather than crumbled. Drizzle with a bit more olive oil, crush more oregano over the feta, and finish with cracked pepper.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Pre-crumbled feta is usually drier and less flavorful. Buy a block of feta in brine (Greek or Bulgarian) and slice it yourself.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Greek tradition: don't toss the salad. Let the feta sit on top; each diner breaks off pieces as they eat.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Let the salad rest at room temperature for 10-15 minutes before serving. Scoop up the olive-oil-and-tomato-juice 'pan sauce' at the bottom with crusty bread — that's the best part.

name: Goma-ae (Sesame Spinach)
description: Blanched spinach dressed in a sweet, nutty sesame sauce with a hint of soy and mirin.
pairingReason: nutty sesame greens to balance grilled proteins

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil and add a generous pinch of salt. While waiting, prepare a large bowl of ice water — this stops the cooking instantly and preserves the spinach's bright green color.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Trim the spinach roots but keep the pink root crown intact — it's the sweetest, most tender part of Japanese spinach.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Goma-ae means 'dressed in sesame' and is one of the most fundamental aemono (dressed dishes) in Japanese home cooking, appearing in nearly every bento box.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Bundle the spinach with a rubber band to keep it together. Submerge the stems in the boiling water first and hold for 20 seconds — stems are thicker and need a head start. Then push the leaves under and blanch for 30 seconds more.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't overblanch — 50 seconds total is enough. Soggy, army-green spinach means you cooked it too long. The leaves should still have a slight bite.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Immediately transfer the spinach to the ice bath and let it cool completely, about 1 minute. This locks in the color and stops the cooking dead.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: If you don't have ice, run the spinach under cold tap water for 30 seconds — slightly less effective but works in a pinch.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Squeeze the spinach firmly in your hands to remove as much water as possible — it should form a compact log. Then cut into 2-inch (5cm) pieces and squeeze each piece again.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: This is the most critical step. Watery spinach dilutes the dressing and makes the dish taste flat. Squeeze until no more water drips out.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Roll the spinach in a clean kitchen towel and twist both ends like a candy wrapper — this gets out more water than squeezing by hand.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Toast 3 tablespoons of white sesame seeds in a dry skillet over medium heat for 2-3 minutes, shaking constantly, until fragrant and just starting to turn golden. Transfer immediately to a mortar or bowl.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Sesame seeds go from toasted to burnt in seconds. Keep the pan moving and pull them off the heat the moment they're fragrant.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: If using store-bought pre-roasted sesame seeds, skip the toasting — they're already done. Just grind them directly.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Grind the toasted sesame seeds in a mortar and pestle (suribachi) until about half are crushed to a paste and half remain whole — this gives you both flavor release and texture. Mix in 1 tablespoon soy sauce and 1 tablespoon sugar until the sugar dissolves.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: No mortar? Put sesame seeds in a zip-lock bag and crush with a rolling pin. You want a rough grind, not a powder.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Add the spinach to the sesame dressing and toss thoroughly until every piece is evenly coated. Divide into small individual portions — goma-ae is traditionally served as a small, elegant side. Serve at room temperature or chilled.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: For a restaurant-style presentation, pack the dressed spinach into a small cup or ramekin, then invert onto the plate for a neat cylinder shape.

name: Hiyayakko (Chilled Tofu)
description: Silky chilled tofu topped with grated ginger, scallions, bonito flakes, and a drizzle of soy sauce.
pairingReason: cool, silky protein with minimal carbs

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Start with a 14 oz (400g) block of silken or soft tofu — it must be silken, not firm. Keep it in the refrigerator until the very last moment. Hiyayakko is best when the tofu is ice-cold.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Using firm or extra-firm tofu ruins this dish. The whole point is the silky, custard-like texture of cold silken tofu. Don't substitute.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: For extra-cold tofu, place the block in a bowl of ice water for 10 minutes before serving.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Hiyayakko dates back to Japan's Edo period (1603–1868), when cooks kept tofu in well water to make it refreshingly cold. The 'yakko' refers to the square shape of a servant's crest.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Drain the tofu gently — wrap the block in a paper towel and let it sit for 5-10 minutes. Don't press it; you want to remove surface water only while keeping the tofu moist and silky inside.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't press the tofu like you would for stir-frying. Pressing silken tofu will crush it and destroy the texture.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: While the tofu drains, prepare the classic toppings: finely slice 2 green onions (scallions), grate 1 tablespoon of fresh ginger using a microplane or the fine side of a box grater, and measure out 2 tablespoons of bonito flakes (katsuobushi).
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Grate ginger across the grain fibers for a smooth paste. Going with the grain gives you stringy, unpleasant pieces.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Cut the cold tofu block into 4 equal portions. Place each piece in an individual small serving bowl or plate — the simplicity of the presentation is part of the experience.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Use a sharp, wet knife to cut silken tofu cleanly. A dry knife drags and tears the surface.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Scoop portions with a large spoon directly from the block if you prefer a more rustic, soft-edged look.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Top each piece with a small mound of sliced scallions, a pinch of grated ginger, and a generous pile of bonito flakes. Serve with soy sauce on the side — each person drizzles their own to taste. Let the toppings sit for 1 minute so the bonito flakes absorb aroma, then serve immediately while ice-cold.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Try ponzu sauce instead of soy sauce for a citrusy twist, or add a drizzle of toasted sesame oil for richness.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: The dancing movement of bonito flakes on hot foods (they wave from the heat) is a beloved visual in Japanese cuisine — on cold tofu, they stay still but add intense umami.

name: Kinpira Gobo (Braised Burdock)
description: Julienned burdock root and carrot stir-fried in soy sauce, mirin, and sesame oil until tender-crisp.
pairingReason: earthy root crunch to ground noodle dishes

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Scrape the skin off 100g (3.5 oz) of burdock root using the back of a knife — don't peel it, as the flavor is concentrated just under the skin. Cut into thin matchsticks about 3mm thick and 5cm (2 inches) long.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Burdock oxidizes (turns brown) fast once cut. Drop the matchsticks into a bowl of cold water immediately as you cut. Don't soak longer than 10 minutes or you'll wash away the flavor.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Many Asian grocery stores sell frozen pre-cut burdock matchsticks — a huge time saver with no quality difference in the finished dish.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Kinpira is named after the legendary folk hero Kintaro's son, Kinpira, who was known for his strength — the dish is said to give you energy and vigor.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Cut 50g (1.75 oz) of carrot into the same thin matchstick shape as the burdock. Slice 1 small dried red chili into thin rings, shaking out the seeds if you want less heat. Mix together the sauce: 1⅓ tablespoons soy sauce, 1 tablespoon each of sake and mirin, and 1 teaspoon sugar.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Cutting the carrot and burdock to identical sizes isn't just aesthetic — it ensures they cook at the same rate.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Heat 1 tablespoon of sesame oil in a skillet over medium-high heat until shimmering. Drain the burdock and add it to the pan. Stir-fry for 2 minutes until the burdock starts to soften and the sesame oil is fragrant.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Use toasted sesame oil for deeper flavor. Regular vegetable oil works but you lose that signature nuttiness.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Add the carrot matchsticks and chili rings. Stir-fry for 30 seconds, then pour in 100ml (about ⅓ cup) of water. Reduce heat to low, cover with a lid, and simmer for 2 minutes until the vegetables are just tender but still have bite.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't overcook — kinpira should have a satisfying crunch. Mushy burdock means you've simmered too long.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Remove the lid, pour in the soy sauce mixture, and increase heat to medium-high. Cook, stirring frequently, for 2-3 minutes until the sauce has almost completely evaporated and the vegetables are glossy and coated.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Keep stirring as the sauce reduces — the sugar can catch and burn on the bottom of the pan if you walk away.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Turn off the heat and sprinkle with toasted white sesame seeds. Let cool slightly — kinpira is traditionally served at room temperature or cold. It keeps beautifully in the fridge for 4-5 days, making it a perfect bento box side.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Make a double batch on Sunday — this is one of the best meal-prep sides in Japanese cooking because the flavor actually improves overnight.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Kinpira is one of Japan's most popular 'joubi-sai' (everyday side dishes) and appears in virtually every bento shop in the country.

name: Sunomono (Cucumber Vinegar Salad)
description: Paper-thin cucumber slices in a tangy rice vinegar dressing with toasted sesame and wakame seaweed.
pairingReason: refreshing acidity to cleanse a rich palate

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Slice 3 Japanese or Persian cucumbers as thin as possible — paper-thin is the goal. A mandoline slicer set to 1-2mm thickness gives the most consistent results, but a sharp knife and patience works too.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Thick slices won't absorb the dressing properly and you'll end up with crunchy, bland cucumber instead of the characteristic tender-crisp, tangy result.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Japanese or Persian cucumbers have thin skins and tiny seeds, so no peeling or seeding needed. Regular English cucumber works but remove the seeds first.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Sunomono literally means 'vinegared things' and is one of the five basic flavor categories (go-mi) in traditional Japanese cuisine.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Sprinkle ¼ teaspoon of salt over the cucumber slices and toss gently with your hands to distribute evenly. Let sit for 5 minutes — the salt draws out excess water so the dressing doesn't get diluted.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't skip the salting step. Watery cucumbers = watery dressing = a bland, disappointing dish. This step is the difference between restaurant-quality and mediocre sunomono.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: After 5 minutes, take handfuls of cucumber slices and squeeze firmly to wring out as much liquid as possible. You'll be surprised how much water comes out — squeeze until your handful feels almost dry.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't be gentle here — really squeeze hard. Limp squeezing leaves too much water and weakens the final flavor.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: In a small bowl, whisk together 3 tablespoons rice vinegar, 1 tablespoon sugar, and ¼ teaspoon soy sauce until the sugar fully dissolves. Taste the dressing — it should be bright, tangy, and slightly sweet.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Use seasoned rice vinegar if you want to skip measuring sugar separately — but taste first, as brands vary in sweetness.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Pour the dressing over the squeezed cucumbers, add 1 teaspoon of sesame seeds, and toss to coat evenly. For the best flavor, refrigerate for at least 15 minutes before serving — the cucumbers absorb the dressing and become even more flavorful.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Add rehydrated wakame seaweed and a splash of toasted sesame oil for a more traditional izakaya-style sunomono.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: In Japanese restaurants, sunomono is often served in small, beautiful ceramic bowls as part of a multi-course meal — its bright acidity is designed to cleanse the palate between richer dishes.

name: Nasu Dengaku (Miso-Glazed Eggplant)
description: Tender roasted eggplant halves glazed with a sweet white miso paste, caramelized under a broiler.
pairingReason: sweet-savory miso depth with every bite

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Slice 2 Japanese eggplants (or 1 globe eggplant) in half lengthwise. Score the cut side in a crosshatch pattern about ¼-inch deep — this helps the flesh cook evenly and gives the miso glaze something to grip onto.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Don't cut too deep with the crosshatch — you want to score the flesh, not slice through to the skin. About ¼ inch is perfect.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Japanese eggplants (long and slender) work best because they're less bitter and more tender than globe eggplants. Look for them at Asian grocery stores.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Dengaku originally referred to a form of Japanese performing art where performers danced on stilts. The skewered tofu and eggplant topped with miso resembled a dengaku dancer on a stick.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Salt the cut sides generously and let sit for 10 minutes. You'll see beads of moisture forming on the surface — this draws out bitterness and excess water. Pat dry thoroughly with paper towels before cooking.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Skipping the salting step with globe eggplant will leave you with bitter, watery results. Japanese eggplants are more forgiving but still benefit from salting.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: While the eggplant rests, make the dengaku miso glaze: whisk together ¼ cup (60g) white or awase miso paste, 2 tablespoons mirin, 1 tablespoon sugar, and 1 tablespoon sake in a small bowl until completely smooth.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: White (shiro) miso gives a sweeter, milder glaze. Red (aka) miso is bolder and saltier. Awase (blended) miso gives you the best of both worlds.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Heat 2 tablespoons of vegetable oil in a large skillet over high heat. Place the eggplant halves skin-side down and cook for 2-3 minutes until the skin is nicely browned and starting to soften.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Eggplant is a sponge for oil. Resist the urge to add more oil — the flesh will release the absorbed oil back as it cooks and softens.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Flip the eggplant halves cut-side down, reduce heat to medium, and cover with a lid. Cook for 3-4 minutes until a chopstick or knife slides through the flesh with zero resistance — the flesh should be creamy and custard-soft throughout.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: Undercooked eggplant is spongy and unpleasant. Don't rush this step — the flesh must be completely soft. If it still feels springy, give it another minute.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Preheat your broiler to high. Place the cooked eggplant halves skin-side down on a foil-lined baking sheet. Brush the dengaku miso glaze generously over the cut surface of each piece — use it all, piling it on thick.
Broil for 3-4 minutes, watching closely, until the miso glaze is bubbling and has caramelized spots of deep golden brown. The glaze should be puffed and aromatic.
guidedCookSteps.mistakeWarning: The sugar in the miso glaze can go from caramelized to burnt in under 30 seconds. Do not walk away — keep the oven light on and watch continuously.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Position the oven rack 6 inches from the broiler element for the best balance of caramelization without burning.

guidedCookSteps.instruction: Remove from the broiler and sprinkle with toasted white sesame seeds. Serve immediately while the glaze is still bubbling — nasu dengaku is at its absolute best when it hits the table hot.
guidedCookSteps.hackChip: Add a tiny pinch of shichimi togarashi (seven-spice) on top for a subtle heat that plays beautifully against the sweet miso.
guidedCookSteps.cuisineFact: Nasu dengaku is a classic izakaya dish — the combination of sweet-savory miso and creamy eggplant is considered one of Japan's most perfect flavor pairings.
